mnemonics.title: Capacity Planning Process Steps
mnemonics.content: S.T.A.R. Framework: 1) Set Goals (define objectives) 2) Track (collect baseline data) 3) Analyze (forecast and model) 4) Recommend (plan and implement)

mnemonics.title: Remember KPIs
mnemonics.content: CAP-KPIs: CPU %, Availability %, Performance metrics, Key metrics, Utilization (Memory/Disk/Network)

mnemonics.title: Resource Types Memory Aid
mnemonics.content: C.M.S.N. - CPU, Memory, Storage, Network are the four core resource types to monitor

acronyms.usage: Contract defining expected service metrics like uptime (99.9%) and response time (under 3 seconds)
Measurable values like CPU utilization, throughput, and response time that demonstrate effectiveness of capacity management
Business metric used to evaluate cost-effectiveness of capacity investments
Isolated computing environment used for efficient capacity utilization through virtualization
Mechanism to guarantee specific performance levels for different workload types

visualTips.visualization: Draw a line graph showing capacity as a horizontal threshold line and demand as a rising curve. The gap between them represents headroom or over-provisioning.
Use a pie chart divided into CPU-bound, I/O-bound, Memory-bound, and Network-bound segments to visualize workload distribution.
Show baseline as a dotted line across a utilization chart. Mark areas above baseline in red (alert zone) and below in green (safe zone).

analogies.analogy: Like a restaurant owner forecasting food supplies - too little means angry customers, too much means wasted inventory and money.
Like wearing properly fitted shoes - too tight hurts (performance issues), too loose wastes money (over-provisioning).
Like a highway's vehicle throughput - lanes per hour determines how many cars can pass, regardless of individual car speed.
Like adding more checkout counters during holiday shopping - either add to existing (vertical) or add new ones (horizontal).
